verses.commentary: Six days before the Passover, Jesus came to Bethany, where Lazarus lived, whom Jesus had raised from the dead. Chronological marker. Passover week begins. Return to Bethany. Lazarus is living proof of resurrection power.

Here a dinner was given in Jesus' honor. Martha served, while Lazarus was among those reclining at the table with Him. Celebration meal. Martha serves (her character). Lazarus reclines (social proof of miracle). Honor to Jesus.

Then Mary took about a pint of pure nard, an expensive perfume; she poured it on Jesus' feet and wiped His feet with her hair. And the house was filled with the fragrance of the perfume. Extravagant worship. Pure nard: costly, imported. Poured, not dabbed. Feet, not head. Hair as towel: shocking intimacy. Fragrance fills house. Worship cannot be hidden.
But one of His disciples, Judas Iscariot, who was later to betray Him, objected. The betrayer named. His objection foreshadows his character. Criticism from inside the circle.
Why wasn't this perfume sold and the money given to the poor? It was worth a year's wages. Economic objection. Three hundred denarii: laborer's annual income. Seemingly reasonable concern for poor.

He did not say this because he cared about the poor but because he was a thief; as keeper of the money bag, he used to help himself to what was put into it. John exposes motive. Judas embezzled. Feigned concern masked greed. Treasurer was thief. Ministry money stolen.
Leave her alone. It was intended that she should save this perfume for the day of My burial. Jesus defends Mary. Burial preparation. She anoints Him for death. Prophetic act. The cross looms.
You will always have the poor among you, but you will not always have Me. Deuteronomy 15:11. Poverty is chronic; His presence temporary. Opportunity for worship now. Economics bow to incarnation.

Meanwhile a large crowd of Jews found out that Jesus was there and came, not only because of Him but also to see Lazarus, whom He had raised from the dead. Curiosity draws crowds. Dual attraction: Jesus and Lazarus. Living evidence. Sightseers and seekers.
So the chief priests made plans to kill Lazarus as well. Eliminate evidence. Lazarus' existence threatens their control. Witnesses to resurrection must be silenced. Murderous logic.

For on account of him many of the Jews were going over to Jesus and believing in Him. Lazarus is evangelistic sign. His life produces faith in Jesus. Testimony through existence. The priests fear losing people.
The next day the great crowd that had come for the festival heard that Jesus was on His way to Jerusalem. Passover pilgrims. News spreads. Messianic expectation builds. Entry anticipated.

They took palm branches and went out to meet Him, shouting, Hosanna! Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord! Blessed is the king of Israel! Palm branches: Feast of Tabernacles symbol, also victory. Hosanna: Save now! Psalm 118:25-26. Messianic acclamation. They proclaim Him king.
Jesus found a young donkey and sat on it, as it is written. Deliberate fulfillment. Zechariah 9:9. Humble mount. King comes peacefully, not on warhorse. Intentional messianic sign.
Do not be afraid, Daughter Zion; see, your king is coming, seated on a donkey's colt. Zechariah 9:9 quoted. Fear not. True king arrives. Gentle, not threatening. Salvation, not conquest.

At first His disciples did not understand all this. Only after Jesus was glorified did they realize that these things had been written about Him and that these things had been done to Him. Initial confusion. Resurrection unlocks understanding. Glorification brings clarity. Hindsight reveals Scripture's fulfillment.

Now the crowd that was with Him when He called Lazarus from the tomb and raised him from the dead continued to spread the word. Eyewitnesses evangelize. Lazarus miracle advertised. Testimony spreads. Word of mouth powerful.
Many people, because they had heard that He had performed this sign, went out to meet Him. Sign draws seekers. Lazarus resurrection motivates crowd. Miracle creates curiosity and faith.

So the Pharisees said to one another, See, this is getting us nowhere. Look how the whole world has gone after Him! Frustration. Admission of failure. Hyperbole: whole world. But prophetic. Gentiles will follow. John 3:16 coming true.
Now there were some Greeks among those who went up to worship at the festival. Gentiles! God-fearers. Proselytes. They come to Jerusalem. Transition begins. Judaism opens to nations.

They came to Philip, who was from Bethsaida in Galilee, with a request. Sir, we would like to see Jesus. Greek names seek Greek-named disciple. Philip from Galilee of the Gentiles. Simple request: see Jesus. Gentile mission beginning.
Philip went to tell Andrew; Andrew and Philip in turn told Jesus. Mediation chain. Disciples facilitate access. They bring Gentiles to Jesus. Apostolic role previewed.
Jesus replied, The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified. The moment. Greeks' arrival signals timing. Universal mission requires cross. Glory through death. The hour is crucifixion.

Very truly I tell you, unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces many seeds. Agricultural metaphor. Death produces life. One seed buried yields many. Jesus' death multiplies disciples. Fruitfulness through sacrifice.

Anyone who loves their life will lose it, while anyone who hates their life in this world will keep it for eternal life. Paradox of discipleship. Self-preservation destroys. Self-sacrifice saves. Hate means relative preference. This world versus eternal life.

Whoever serves Me must follow Me; and where I am, My servant also will be. My Father will honor the one who serves Me. Service means following. Disciples go where Jesus goes: cross, then glory. Father honors faithful servants. Divine recognition for human faithfulness.

Now My soul is troubled, and what shall I say? Father, save Me from this hour? No, it was for this very reason I came to this hour. Gethsemane foreshadowed. Psuchē (ψυχή) troubled. Tarasso (ταράσσω), deep agitation. Temptation to escape. But purpose demands the cross. For this He came.

Father, glorify Your name! Then a voice came from heaven, I have glorified it, and will glorify it again. Prayer for Father's glory. Divine response. Audible voice. Past glory: ministry. Future glory: cross and resurrection. God speaks publicly.

The crowd that was there and heard it said it had thundered; others said an angel had spoken to Him. Varied perception. Some hear thunder. Others angel. Few recognize God's voice. Revelation meets human limitation.
Jesus said, This voice was for your benefit, not Mine. Voice for their sake. Jesus needs no audible confirmation. Public revelation for witnesses. They need to hear God validate Him.

Now is the time for judgment on this world; now the prince of this world will be driven out. The cross is judgment day. Satan's defeat. Ruler of this world cast out. Cosmic battle engaged. Victory through apparent defeat.

And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to Myself. Lifted up: crucifixion. John's double meaning (3:14, 8:28). Exaltation through execution. All people: Jews and Gentiles. Universal magnetic power. The cross attracts.
He said this to show the kind of death He was going to die. John explains. Lifted up means crucifixion. Manner of death indicated. Roman execution method.

The crowd spoke up, We have heard from the Law that the Messiah will remain forever, so how can You say, The Son of Man must be lifted up? Who is this Son of Man? Objection. Scripture says Messiah eternal (Psalm 89:36, Isaiah 9:7, Daniel 7:14). How can He die? Confusion over titles and timing.

Then Jesus told them, You are going to have the light just a little while longer. Walk while you have the light, before darkness overtakes you. Whoever walks in the dark does not know where they are going. Urgency. Limited time. Use the light now. Darkness coming. Blindness without light. Disorientation in darkness.

Believe in the light while you have the light, so that you may become children of light. When He had finished speaking, Jesus left and hid Himself from them. Final appeal. Believe now. Become light bearers. He departs. Concealment. Public ministry effectively ends. The hour approaches.

Even after Jesus had performed so many signs in their presence, they still would not believe in Him. Summary statement. Many signs. Persistent unbelief. Evidence abundant. Faith absent. Hardness of heart.

This was to fulfill the word of Isaiah the prophet: Lord, who has believed our message and to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed? Isaiah 53:1. Rejection prophesied. Unbelief is ancient pattern. Messianic suffering foretold and ignored.
For this reason they could not believe, because, as Isaiah says elsewhere. Divine hardening. Not just would not but could not. Judicial blindness. God's sovereignty in unbelief.

He has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts, so they can neither see with their eyes, nor understand with their hearts, nor turn, and I would heal them. Isaiah 6:10. God blinds and hardens. Not arbitrary but judicial response to persistent rejection. They cannot see, understand, or turn. Healing unavailable to hardened hearts.
Isaiah said this because he saw Jesus' glory and spoke about Him. Isaiah's vision (Isaiah 6) was of Christ. Pre-incarnate glory. Old Testament prophets saw Jesus. Unity of Scripture.

Yet at the same time many even among the leaders believed in Him. But because of the Pharisees they would not openly acknowledge their faith for fear they would be put out of the synagogue. Secret believers. Leaders included. Fear silences confession. Excommunication threat. Social cost of faith. Covert discipleship.
For they loved human praise more than praise from God. Root issue. Love hierarchy wrong. Human approval valued over divine approval. Fear of man versus fear of God. Misplaced affection.
Then Jesus cried out, Whoever believes in Me does not believe in Me only, but in the One who sent Me. Final public declaration. Belief in Jesus is belief in the Father. Unity of mission and identity.
The one who looks at Me is seeing the One who sent Me. Visual revelation. Seeing Jesus is seeing God. Incarnation makes invisible visible. John 1:18, 14:9.

I have come into the world as a light, so that no one who believes in Me should stay in darkness. Mission statement. Light brings escape from darkness. Belief transfers from darkness to light. Salvation as illumination.

If anyone hears My words but does not keep them, I do not judge that person. For I did not come to judge the world, but to save the world. First coming: salvation. Jesus does not judge now. Mission is rescue. Judgment deferred.

There is a judge for the one who rejects Me and does not accept My words; the very words I have spoken will condemn them at the last day. Future judgment. Jesus' words are the standard. Final day brings reckoning. Rejection brings condemnation. The word judges.

For I did not speak on My own, but the Father who sent Me commanded Me to say all that I have spoken. Divine origin of teaching. Father commanded. Jesus obeyed. Words are God's words. Authority derived.

I know that His command leads to eternal life. So whatever I say is just what the Father has told Me to say. Father's command brings life. Jesus speaks exactly what Father says. Perfect transmission. Faithful messenger. Life through obedience to the word.
